codeReview.summary: The commit adds a real hero visual stack (provider, WebGL canvas, ASCII background, SSR-safe mounting, and tests), but it does not fully satisfy the feature's fallback requirements. Unsupported `matchMedia` environments can still break the hero outright, and the WebGL path only falls back on initialization failures, not on runtime context loss after the shader has started.

issues.description: `useReducedMotion` calls `window.matchMedia(...)` during state initialization and again in the effect without checking that the API exists or that `addEventListener` is supported. In browsers/webviews that lack that API surface, the hero crashes before any visual fallback can render, which violates the requirement that unsupported environments fail cleanly.

Runtime fallback is only implemented for initialization-time failures (`getContext`, shader compile/link). After successful startup the canvas never listens for `webglcontextlost`/similar runtime failure signals, so a lost GPU/WebGL context leaves the dead shader layer mounted instead of switching to the CSS fallback path promised by `VAL-VISUAL-004`.

The hero still keeps much of the preexisting generic shell styling (`backdrop-blur-sm` cards, secondary-colored body copy, and `rounded-md` CTAs at lines 55-100), so the feature lands new background layers but only partially upgrades the first viewport into the claimed signature BETTER/HFT visual system.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The mission shared state is internally inconsistent on ASCII. The feature brief still demands an ASCII-video-inspired background, while the mission AGENTS and the frontend skill both say ASCII layers are permanently removed and the approved stack is Radiant + film grain. That contradiction directly encouraged this worker to add `AsciiBackground.tsx` and still report `followedProcedure=true`.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: features.json:549-561 requires an ASCII-video-inspired background; mission AGENTS.md:37-38 and 113-128 ban ASCII and require a single Radiant shader plus film grain; frontend-experience-worker/SKILL.md:26-27 repeats the ASCII ban.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Procedure compliance is not being reported reliably for hero/immersive work. The skill requires design-system/screenshot review plus stronger enhanced-state browser evidence, but this transcript goes from README/testing/user-testing/content-model reads straight into implementation and the handoff only proves the headless fallback path. `skillFeedback.followedProcedure=true` should not have been accepted as-is.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: SKILL.md:24-25 and 49-51 require design-system/research review and explicit enhanced-state evidence; the transcript skeleton for session 3304a1bf-dad9-440e-af39-e2c38b51844c shows reads of testing.md, user-testing.md, and content-model.md before edits, but no design-system.md or screenshot review; the handoff notes only that headless Chrome lacked WebGL and exercised fallback.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Browser-validation service usage is documented but not enforced. This worker started `npm run dev` manually for validation even though the shared state already defines a production-backed `web` service and warns validators away from the raw dev-server path.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: services.yaml:9-13 defines the `web` service on port 3100; .factory/library/user-testing.md:40-43 says to use the manifest-backed service and rerun headed if headless reports `webglSupport=false`; the transcript skeleton shows `HOSTNAME=127.0.0.1 PORT=3100 npm run dev -- --hostname 127.0.0.1 --port 3100`.

summary: Review failed. The feature added the intended hero visual components and test coverage, but two blocking gaps remain in the fallback story: unsupported `matchMedia` environments can still crash the hero, and post-init WebGL failures never transition to the fallback path. I also recorded shared-state inconsistencies around stale ASCII guidance, weak skill-procedure reporting, and unenforced service usage.
